segments.text: No other setup has made more money on the desk than this strategy.
And the RSI indicator is by far the most widely used indicator for nailing this setup.
The desk just made a huge trade, a massive run followed by a dramatic crack overnight.
We traded ourselves.
The story behind this trade is wild, and the RSI was crucial.
Not all trades within a strategy are created equally known.
But why does some traders fail to see the difference?
We use RSI to spot the signal.
And a big part of this is how we customize the settings.
We'll get into all of this.
But Tim, when did you start thinking about this big silver show?
I would say it was the week of Christmas.
So Monday was the 22nd.
I think SLV was getting above 60 at that point.
And that's when we started thinking that this could be a big trade.
Right. And where were you at this point?
I was in Wester, Massachusetts, Friday.
For when we actually traded it, I was in Rhode Island to start the week with back home with my parents.
And Friday, I was Friday at the close.
I was driving the back roads of Vermont up to Skietto, Kimo.
So crazy.
What about you?
Did you have FOMO at all?
Like just being in the car, being on the slopes during this whole thing?
When I had service sites of FOMO.
But that was where I know that we had our team looking at this.
And we were executing for this trade as well.
So that helps with the FOMO.
It does.
And I mean, that just kind of speaks to the power of being on a team, right?
As you can kind of pick up the slack if somebody is off doing something.
And you were still communicating with us about this trade.
So you still had input.
You just weren't pressing the buttons.
I was texted on Discord.
Yeah, right.
I was at my in-laws house because this happened over Christmas.
This is why we were all gone.
And I was pressing buttons on my laptop in my in-laws living room when I.
I did some sir.
I think Maggie's dad was like watching the Eagles game or something like that.
And I'm just zoned in on the silver chart.
So, all right.
So what was this trade?
This was this was a big silver short.
So we're expressing with SLV, right?
What was this trade?
So SLV broke out the week of Thanksgiving in a major fashion broke out of a huge weekly base.
SLV broke out Thanksgiving week.
And then this trade happened Christmas week.
Both holiday weeks is just funny.
It is funny because it was literally Thanksgiving.
It was when it broke out.
It was either the day after the Friday.
I think it was I was I was in my dad's kitchen on my laptop again.
Trading that one.
Just holiday trade.
So yeah, it broke out over Thanksgiving and then topped over Christmas.
So this is a.
This is a big trade for us.
Like I mean, everyone has different playbooks and you know certain trades are bigger than others.
But for our entire desk.
This is a really, really big playbook that a lot of people on our desk trade well.
How would you define this playbook?
But I would define it when you get a stock that is overextended on multiple time frames.
You are then looking for a potential top and a blow off as we call it.
And you're looking to capture that backside.
Okay, so it's a short trade after something is run.
And an unusual way, right?
So yes, I would classify it as an outlier situation where something gets extraordinarily extended and then and then you look for the short.
And it's falls under the mean reversion playbook.
So we've talked about trend following and past episodes.
This is now mean reversion.
Right.
We did we did talk about the gold trade.
And I forget which episode but one of them in which we covered a trade that was within this playbook.
Both metals, which is kind of funny because that's the kind of year it's been.
It's been the year of the commodities.
Yeah.
So.
All right.
So this was a this was a mean version short in silver.
It happened over Christmas.
And to me, I mean, this happened in a very unusual way because it was over Christmas.
A lot happened overnight.
It wasn't the top was actually overnight.
So there's such a big event on the daily chart.
I mean, days leading up to this thing that it was running every single day.
I think it closed at the highs on Friday and then ripped into the futures open on Monday or Sunday night rather and then just flushed hard into the open and then throughout that Monday when we open.
All right.
Let's let's slow it down a little bit.
Garrett.
Let's look at the chart.
So what was the actual trade?
What did we take?
What do we execute on?
Yeah.
So Friday.
We saw that silver sped up and closed at the highs.
And that's the second to last candle on this chart.
So you can see that big green candle and just notice like the trend and in the speed up of the trend is really important.
And then we got that gap up and that huge run on Friday in a close of the highs.
And so we're thinking we're getting really close.
Right.
This is this is getting to the point in the trade where this is getting so extended that we want to find some way to express a trade.
Would you like to gain the biggest edge of retail trader can get?
All of our daily and weekly in house trader meetings are now available to you.
Just head over to SMBTrainingFloric.com to find out more.
And you mentioned RSI.
What's interesting with this setup, this parabolic short, the overextended short.
RSI is a huge tool because a lot of the times and you can see it since the breakout from Thanksgiving.
We'll hear on the desk and you'll just hear from traders in general where they're like it's still too early.
And there's a lot of merit to that like saying that is really really significant.
But what was what was different on this Friday for you?
Yeah, so I'll tell you a little story about that.
So I host these bionic trader meetings on Tuesdays.
And we go over signals like this, right?
Like the RSI, like how we on the desk use the RSI.
We talk about some of the indicators that we've developed and we share them.
And we were going over silver.
So this would be Tuesday.
We're talking about putting the trade on Friday.
So this was four days before the top before we put that trade on.
And I was outlining how we were seeing this as a potential really great mean reversion setup.
And we were talking about what a big opportunity this could become.
And as we looked at all the variables, we decided that it was early.
Though we really like this trade, we would love to see it extend more and speed up more before we really put on the risk.
And so that is something that we say a lot, right?
We might love a trade, but we might say it's early.
And so fast forward to Friday, what changed was the acceleration of this move.
So it was already up a lot.
It was already late stage in this move.
But what we really wanted to see was the speed up.
We wanted to see it extend.
And this is something that the RSI can measure.
And we have a specific way of using it to actually capture that acceleration, which you can't totally see in the RSI on this chart.
But we'll show that in a second.
We were looking at Keltner channels, which is ATRs above a moving average and just going to historical examples of S.L.V.
Silver, gold, other metals in other regimes where the trend got out of hand and it's fed up and went parabolic.
And we were looking at those shorts and looking at what those indicators look like at those moments in comparing it to here.
And one of the interesting things that I find about this trade is that it's silver.
It's a metal, right?
And we talked about gold, same thing.
These commodities can act a little bit different than stocks.
And that's always in the back of my mind when I'm trading these because...
Do you want to sit in on all of our daily and weekly in-house trader meetings?
There's no bigger edge retail traders can get.
Visit smbtradingfloor.com to learn more.
Stocks tend to blow off really fast, right?
As soon as they get out of hand, it might be options driven or whatever.
These commodities can go on a lot further than we think.
I mean, so can stocks, but commodities really tend to do that, especially like...
I mean, stuff like natural gas, what do they call it?
Like the Widowmaker?
Because they just have drivers behind them that can create these squeezes.
So I just thought that on Friday, when we get that close, after a big gap,
where the range expands, and you can see that on the chart, you know,
we're thinking it's getting very close now.
It's no longer early.
It might gap up, but if it does, that's even better.
And at that point, this is becoming an A plus setup.
In another way, even to say, a lot of people like to use the analogy of a rubber band for this setup,
where you can only stretch a rubber band so far before it snaps back.
And commodities, you can maybe stretch it a little bit more than certain stock squeezes.
So on Tuesday, that was when it was really starting to peak our interest.
And at that point, I think we were talking with our team,
and we were like, this might have a couple more days left.
Yeah, absolutely.
And I mean, for that, I would just say, with a trade like this, context is everything.
We have our ways of getting involved.
We have our entry day setups and things like that that might, you know,
our opening range breaks and things like that that might tell us that this trade is triggered.
But we need a really great context for a trade like this.
And by context, I just mean some overall environment filter that says yellow light green light, right?
Where that green light is like, okay, now we're looking for the trade.
Because it's very easy to get run over.
And it's very easy to be trying this on the front side too early and just getting chopped up and chopped up and chopped up.
That really is the risk of this trade.
And so in my opinion, you have to have ways of keeping that in check and measuring these things in order to define that context.
And that's really where like these indicators come into play for something like this.
I completely agree.
So, you know, as Tim, you and I both have done a lot of quant work, right?
And that's something that we have a lot of experience in.
And so because of that, we've dealt with a lot of testing of strategies and a lot of variables.
Because when you're testing a strategy, it really forces you to quantify market dynamics.
Because you start to realize that you can't tell an algorithm, hey, like when this gets out of hand, short it.
Or when this gets out of hand, give me a heads up.
When this, when it becomes up too much, let me know, right?
Like you have to define up too much.
There's no other way around it.
And so now all of a sudden, you're thinking about how can I quantify that?
And you're either inventing metrics that will describe what you want to see,
or you're using perhaps existing indicators like the RSI to define that.
And so I think a lot of the times discretionary traders sort of lose this point.
Because we're so busy as discretionary traders getting a feel for things and having intuition
that we start to lose the fact that we're able to use data to quantify things like the context.
And it's really important in a trade like this.
Like so what about this particular setup to you forces us to quantify?
Just like you said with a model, just saying it's up too much does not give you the conviction.
And similar to the clockwork we're doing, like using trade data as we call it,
discretionary version of that is just checks in favor.
More checks in favor means this is a better setup.
So as a discretionary trader, you're looking for many checks in favor to tell you that this is up too much.
Right. Yeah. So I mean, we, I've done some videos on YouTube.
You know, because of the Quantwork we've done, I've done a number of videos about indicators.
And one thing that I notice is that in the comments when you talk about indicators,
you get two camps of people.
Right. I know there are people in the team with the people that tend to comment are either saying,
thank you for providing the answer to all of my problems.
Now I can make millions of dollars.
This is what I've been looking for.
Or it's indicators or the particular indicator you're talking about has no edge.
It's lagging. Like I only use charts. I only use chart patterns.
I pride myself in that your indicators are trash.
And for some reason, and I don't know why it awakens such a polarizing stance.
Right. Do you sure does say it?
And so who is right?
I mean, out of both parties, first off, yeah, they're armies on both sides.
And I would argue they're both not right.
The ones that say this indicator is the lord and savior.
I would argue that just one indicator alone will not help you trade every single setup.
And a full diverse playbook. And I would argue on the other end that if you're thinking an indicator is lagging,
we'll get into what even is an indicator because that's a whole different story.
If you think I'm never going to use an indicator, it's lagging.
You're missing out on a ton of information that the market gives you every second.
Right. I mean, indicators are not strategies.
Right. They're just tools. They're data points.
They're ways for us to describe variables of these strategies.
And everything's lagging. Everything's historical data.
Like even your awesome work on fundamental analysis, Tim,
is stuff that everybody knows or can know, right?
Because it's out. It's out there.
Right. Chart chart data is historical.
Like I would argue that candlestick charts.
Is an indicator. High low open, close historical data of high low open, close.
We didn't always have that.
Can you imagine trading back in 1929, you're Jesse Livermore.
You're just that you're reading the ticker tape as the as it's coming out of the machine.
Looking at the quotes.
And then someone comes in with a computer and shows you candlestick charts of the historical price data
and explains this is the high low open, close of all of these time periods.
Like check out this indicator I created.
You'd be like, this is wild.
I'm going to use.
It told me Jesse Livermore didn't use MACD.
Yeah, exactly. So, you know, I think in a way, like I like to think of it as like
a broadening the idea of indicators, right?
The fact that like kind of any data that we look at in a way is an indicator.
And it's not just, you know, you're your classic RSI and MACD.
I mean, Paul Tutor Jones, like what did he do?
How what was he doing?
Yeah, I mean, that's kind of fun.
What's the thing about it?
Like, what was he doing 70s and 80s if he saw an overextended short like silver?
Like it could have been in corn futures.
But so he didn't he didn't use indicators like we're talking about RSI.
So there's a great YouTube movie.
I think it's probably still on YouTube.
I forget what it's called, but you can probably just.
Yeah, you just type in Paul Tutor Jones movie or something like that.
And it follows him around during the 1987 crash.
It's it's gold.
And I mean, you get all the vintage 80s vibes in there, but you get to see him on the phone.
You get to see him working with his assistant.
And this is right in the thick of it.
I think this was right before the crash and they're talking about, I think the market's still running up.
If I remember correctly.
He gets with his assistant and his assistant has printed out all of these analogs to 1987.
So I think they had the big one was 1929.
And they were taking the charts and they were super imposing them.
And comparing the run in 1929 to the run in 1987 and saying the same stuff that we've been talking about, which is I think we're close.
Because they're they're looking at the slope of the chart and trying to figure out like it does this.
Is this a check in favor?
Does this qualify for something that is going parabolic that is going to blow off?
And they were there.
I think they were long at the time, but they were they were looking for the end.
So he was looking at his playbook.
Maybe it was the Friday right before that Monday.
And he was like, this is pretty similar in terms of an extension.
All right.
I agree.
But it's in the way that's just indicators back then too.
It was the candlestick data.
Yeah.
So indicators are just math.
It's just the testics.
You know, they're not traders.
They're not strategies.
So we just think of them in a black and white fashion.
If we want the data, we get the data.
That's that's all it is.
And so it's really important to understand in my opinion what your indicators are measuring and.
And the nuances of them and when they work and when they don't.
Can you think of instances Tim where indicators are dangerous like using a certain indicator might be.
Completely the wrong thing to do.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I have a couple.
I would say one of them would be a strategy that's just based on.
Shorting RSI of 70 or buying RSI when it drops to 30.
Another example.
I would even say is with the moving averages where we only look at moving averages.
Certain moments when stocks are doing certain things.
And other times they're completely useless.
Like if a stock's been in a range for a year or let's say even a month range.
It's been a month range.
Looking at the moving averages and trying to play like any moving average cross.
Is just going to lead to you getting chopped up and taking a million paper cuts.
Okay.
So there are.
There are certain signals that you don't like.
For instance, the RSI on those default settings, which we can which we're going to get into.
Or certain indicators have environments where they work and where they don't.
I totally agree with you about the moving average thing because that's a trend.
If we're not in a trend, the moving average is going to be meaningless.
How do you like to use indicators because.
You don't always have to just use it for an entry signal.
Like what are some of the ways that you like to use them?
Honestly, I yeah, I really really don't use them for entry signals signals at all.
Indicators for me really just play into the context.
And we look at not just a single indicator like we mentioned.
But we have ones that we're always looking at on our charts.
And I use them to just give context for the playbook trades.
So they're not trades alone like we mentioned.
But the ones I like to use RSI, the moving averages.
Looking at the Keltner's and all that's doing is telling me more about the stock and where it's at where it's position compared to the market.
And it's the context.
But I would never I would never honestly trade just off of it alone.
And even our models like man, we don't have been trade off one like we have multiple entry criteria.
I think another great way to use them is for filtering because you know you can like someone might say, oh I can just see it in the chart.
And that's great. I think that's true.
Like you know, especially if you can do that if you have those skills which I think you know you should.
Then that's a that's true almost 100% of the time.
But at the same time if you were to say well then if you can see it in the chart show me the 10 stocks in the market that are the most overextended right now.
How you can see that in the chart you've got to create some filter for that so you can use something like the RSI or some input to get a list.
So I think that's one of my favorite ways to use them.
And in this case we're looking at outlier situations.
So I like to use indicators to spot outlier scenarios.
Some people like to trade off indicators with stocks doing normal stock things.
And that's fine.
I like to set my indicators so that they're very extreme.
And we're going to do this with the RSI in a second because this is this is how we use it.
But I like to have my settings in such a way that it's going to alert me to something that is very rare.
And in that case it's more meaningful to me when I see it and it's not just so much noise like I'm looking at this thing all the time and there's all these signals and then I have to decide.
If the signal's meaningful or not, I'm kind of just following the price action looking at the tape and then I kind of have my indicators and every once in a while.
It's like whoa, okay, like this is this is this is crazy. This is worth my attention.
It makes a lot of sense.
Do you ever use them for anything like just what's normal price action per se or?
I would say my favorite thing to use that is just a normal cycle is just that volatility cycle that we use which is anyone who has used or heard of the TTM squeeze.
It's basically looking at when a stock is super compressed and that happens all the time stocks get compressed.
But it's a great way to understand the stage that the stock isn't and how much energy it has.
But I'm always combining that with the price action in the news and whether it's a leader and you know whether the chart looks good right.
So it's not standing on its own.
Have you heard this study of tens of millions of traders which shows that only one percent of retail traders actually make it?
Don't be a statistic.
Visit smvtradingfloor.com to greatly increase your odds.
Real quick, I don't want to dive too much back into the whole indicators are just math.
But I mean the wall cycle is something that you created.
You know, you've done a ton of work with indicators and that might be a good example of how you really can customize and create your own indicators.
And what you're doing is a lot of math.
Yeah, I think the signal better.
I mean it's fun and like we started doing it because of the quant stuff and then and then you kind of find that certain things have value.
So you bring it into the real world and maybe making a word or something like that.
So with this silver trade.
Like what are we trying to quantify?
Like if we just if no indicators existed.
And you had to tell me like I need an alert or I need I need an indicator that tells me X, Y and Z.
Like what are we trying to define here?
We're really trying to define when it becomes overextended.
So like when it becomes so strong and so just so unusually strong that it's unsustainable.
But what's interesting like our aside is what gauges that strength.
And what we're really have to with this overextended short and like what makes one of trades an A plus trade is when it becomes that this move is unsustainable.
Like it can't be at these prices anymore.
I would classify that as acceleration.
I would look at.
We can have a stock that's up a lot and Lance does a great job talking about this.
And it's so true.
We can have a stock that's up a lot on a percent basis or up a lot over a quarter.
But if it's just grinding in a linear fashion.
That means it's price accepting and going higher and price accepting and going higher.
And nothing's really changing. It's just trending.
So what edge do we really have to step in and short that?
Yeah, the plate, the slope of price is one.
That is just a normal trend.
Right. So when something is up a lot, it's trending.
It's trending. It is getting extended.
What we really want to see is not only something that has high velocity, but high acceleration.
We want to see the parabolic nature of that price path increase.
And that's really just euphoria, right?
That's shorts getting blown out.
That's longs getting exuberant in the late stage of the cycle.
That's people who have been long for maybe the whole move are starting to think,
my gosh, like I should take profits.
It's like a perfect storm.
It's so extended from those moving averages that now all of a sudden the risk reward,
like the meat on the bone for that pullback becomes greater and short.
Short will step in like on our desk.
And we get that snapback, which tends to be quite violent.
And I would say like something on this silver, like we're just looking for a day trade.
Like we're not you're not trying to swing this right?
Like do you have a fundamental view of silver?
Like it should be done.
No, in first that's also when you have people on Twitter saying if you're getting long silver up here,
you're insane.
Like everyone on Twitter was like, how could you be getting long up here?
Almost like mocking them.
But yeah, it's a very different thing when it comes to commodities.
When it comes to silver, the gold that we talked about where they do have some fundamental driver.
Maybe they overshot and that's why we're looking for this trade.
But there is some reason behind the move.
And in a lot of cases with this setup, there isn't.
Like it could be a company that has zero revenue.
Isn't changing the world not involved in AI?
Sounds like beyond me.
Is beyond me.
And what that gets into though is you can see it in certain stocks.
When they make that crazy of a move, that extended of a move, RSI gets above 80.
It gets above 90.
And this is really worth a dollar or worth zero.
It becomes so unsustainable where it can't, it really can't sustain at these prices.
So you're more likely to see this short setup, I would say in those.
But it's a different, it's a different thing because they are not supposed to be up there.
And they may never get up there again.
Whereas, you know, something like gold like we covered in another episode in this silver.
Like they've got major macro drivers behind them.
And you know, there's a, there's a bigger force at play.
And we're not, we're not trying to be analysts and think far out and say, you know, this is, this is it for forever.

We're just trying to make a day trade based on the technical dynamics of this trade and the same technical dynamics that play out on silver play out on something like beyond meat that might be overvalued.
The difference is that I personally, I would be much more comfortable swinging that short and beyond or something that I know got out of hand, even just dislocated from its value.
Whereas, I mean, with these metals, like, I'm not a macro expert.
I know that there are a lot of fundamental drivers behind these things.
For all I know, silver gathers itself and goes higher. I mean, we shorted gold.
I don't know when was that October or something.
Yeah.
And that was another kind of day trade for me.
And what happened? Well, it did come off and then it consolidated and then made a new all time high.
So I don't know a couple of months later.
So well, in the start of the moves like for gold and silver, it's like central banks buying.
And like, and there's a ton like you said, other drivers.
Yeah.
But when you have a beyond meat going insane or any random low flow going insane, it's less of there's a ton of people buying this.
It's a liquidity trap.
It's a short squeeze in a major way where it's not just fresh buying.
It's shorts then having to buy stock to cover their positions.
And you get those squeeze dynamics.
So it's a very different setup.
And at the same time, like you said, you can trade them the exact same way.
And the technicals are very similar.
So with the desk of made millions of dollars on this trade, if it weren't for RSI.
That's a pretty, that's a pretty interesting question.
So the answer is yes.
I, I 100% think they would have because it's not as great.
RSI is the most popular indicator for being able to see when it's up too much.
But by no means is it the only indicator.
And for the desk, there are a lot of other ways that we could have looked at silver and thought this is ready.
So you can look at the number of days up.
You can look at, we already mentioned kind of the Keltner channels.
You can look at, you know, how far it is from key moving averages.
The extension gaps.
But there's multiple ways that you could see it.
And I mean, speaking of seeing it, you could even see it with your own eye.
Which, you know, does come from seeing a ton of examples marking these up.
But you could even just do the eye test and have an idea.
So I would say the answer is yeah.
Yeah, and I mean, what do you think?
Yeah, the RSI happens to be the most popular indicator used for this setup.
And it happens to be highly effective.
So I think it's great.
But I think to that whole indicator argument that we had before where you have your polarized views.
You know, there are many ways to scan a cat.
So I agree.
There are other indicators that do the same thing.
And you just have to know your indicators.
And that's what we'll go over this in second.
And Tim, I want to show you the custom settings that I like to use.
But you can, you know, and I like to, I always have to see the chart with my eyes.
And feel that acceleration.
Understand what the tape feels like.
And I think that's something to be said for.
You're sitting there.
Every day, like Tuesday, when I'm doing bionic trader, I'm talking about what a big op this can be.
And I'm having to restrain myself from shorting it.
Right?
Like I'm thinking this is, this is getting close.
This is really, this could be really good.
What if I miss it?
Should we put this trade on now that we just did?
Right?
And so for me, it's not that like I'm just looking at something like the RSI.
And it's the only way to do it.
But in those moments, you can lean on those indicators to kind of keep you and check.
Like you said earlier, checks in favor.
What we didn't have certain checks in favor with the indicators that we normally do.
And so that just gives me that extra little objective perspective on the whole thing to kind of calm me down and say, okay, like let's be.
Let's be reasonable here.
It's early, right?
And just follow the process.
So I think I think these things can be really, really helpful for that kind of exercise.
I like to look at these on multiple time frames.
So there's two keys to why this becomes effective.
And I see this happen on the desk.
These three things that we're going to do.
We're going to change the settings.
We're going to change two settings and we're going to use multiple time frames.
And I see this across the desk.
It's not just the RSI signal on the daily.
Right?
It's the RSI signal on the daily plus some intermediate time frame.
Like the hourly or like I use the 130 minute chart.
And then it's on a shorter time frame, like the 30 minute chart.
Right?
So we want to see these signals line up across time frames.
And we don't want to follow the default settings.
That everyone gets on their platforms on the RSI because they're way too normal.
What do you mean by what are the default settings?
So it's a 14 period.
That's the classic thing.
And for the look back, you're saying.
Yeah.
And the overbought is 70 and the oversold is 30.
That's those are the thresholds.
So the RSI is an oscillator that is bound between zero and a hundred.
And it's measuring the rate of change though the amount of up moves that are in those candles.
In that look back period and the amount of down moves that are in that in the candles during that look back period.
Right?
And it's calculating all of that and creating an oscillator where.
